reasoning: Historically, U.S. regular gas prices exhibit a clear seasonal pattern: they tend to rise through early-to-mid summer (peaking around July/August due to peak driving demand and summer-grade fuel costs) before declining significantly in the autumn and reaching their annual lows in winter. The most recent FRED data combined with AAA's current reports indicate a period of relative stability with minor upward pressure heading into late June 2025. Because the initial baseline date (June 22) is right before the peak summer season, there is a good chance prices will be slightly higher by late June and July. Therefore, the probabilities for the first two horizons (June 29 and July 22) are set above 0.5. By September and December, the strong seasonal downward pressure makes a net increase less likely, so the probabilities fall below 0.5. However, as instructed, forecast uncertainty increases strictly over time, pulling the probabilities for the more distant horizons monotonically closer to 0.5. Long-term volatility in global crude oil prices over a 6-month horizon provides fundamental justification for pulling the December estimate very close to a coin toss instead of anchoring solely on the winter seasonal drop.
